lines.text: You don't have to say anything. Just rest. I'll be right here when you wake up.

lines.text: You saved my life! You are my brother! And no distance, no war, no winter will ever change that! Go! Live!

direction.design: A grown woman in her late thirties with a full, rich, powerful adult voice — a stage actress, not childlike, in American English.
direction.instruct: Shout with heightened passion bordering on forceful aggression — but the emotion is fierce LOVE and pride, not anger; voice breaking with devotion.

lines.text: They said we could not build it. They said we would not last the winter. Well, look around you — we are still here!

direction.instruct: A leader delivering the climax of a rousing speech to a crowd — ringing, forceful, rhythmic, sweeping the listeners up, in American English.

lines.text: I have seen what we can be when we stand together, and I will not — I cannot — let that dream die tonight!

lines.text: The library opened at nine, but Mrs. Aldery arrived at eight, as she had for eleven years, to sort the returns and water the fern that nobody else remembered.

lines.text: Hush now, little sailor, the harbor lights are low. Sleep now, little sailor, the tide will rock you slow.

direction.design: A warm adult woman's voice, soft and close, in American English.
direction.instruct: Sing this gently as a slow lullaby to a child — soft, melodic, rocking rhythm, almost a whisper-song.

lines.text: Close your eyes, my little wren, the moon will mind the garden. Dream away the day again, the night will keep you warm.

lines.text: Oh, my heart, my heart, she is gone over the sea, and the waves will not carry her back to me.

direction.design: A grown woman's voice, worn and sorrowful, in American English.
direction.instruct: Keen this as a wailed lament, half sung and half wept — a rising and falling mourning song, grief carried on melody.

lines.text: By stone and by star, we hold the line. By root and by river, this land is ours to keep.

direction.instruct: Chant this as a solemn rhythmic vow — measured, resonant, on the border between speech and song, like an oath sworn aloud, in American English.

lines.text: One voice, one fire, one unbroken circle. We remember, we remain, we rise.

lines.text: Oh what a day, what a wonderful day, the kettle is singing and so am I!

direction.design: A grown woman in her twenties with a bright, warm, fully adult voice — playful but natural, NOT cartoonish, in American English.
direction.instruct: Speak in a light sing-song lilt, musical and skipping, humming the words more than saying them.

lines.text: Then Marcus jabbed his stick at the ice and growled, "This net is mine tonight. All mine." And nobody argued.

direction.design: A warm adult female audiobook narrator, in American English.
direction.instruct: Narrate warmly; when quoting the teenage boy Marcus, lower and roughen your voice to play him, then return to your own voice for the rest.

lines.text: Gran leaned in, eyes twinkling, and rasped, "I buried three husbands, dear. I can certainly handle one raccoon." Then she picked up the broom.

direction.design: A warm adult female audiobook narrator, in American English.
direction.instruct: Narrate warmly; when quoting Gran, soften and age your voice into a raspy twinkle to play her, then return to your own voice for the rest.

lines.text: Maya tugged his sleeve and squeaked, "But what if the stars get lonely up there?" He had no answer for that.

direction.instruct: Narrate warmly; when quoting the small girl Maya, raise and lighten your voice to play her, then return to your own voice for the rest.

lines.text: The innkeeper, a mountain of a man, rumbled, "We don't serve wizards here. Not since the incident." His mustache bristled at the memory.

direction.design: A warm adult female audiobook narrator, in American English.
direction.instruct: Narrate warmly; when quoting the huge innkeeper, drop into a deep gravelly rumble to play him, then return to your own voice for the rest.
